Scaling
Savage x Fenty
--------------

Unlike Fenty Beauty, Savage x Fenty=E2=80=99s first-mover status into large=
r-size intimate
apparel did not make it an instant success. The venture-backed TechStyle=E2=
=80=99s membership model (users pay a monthly fee in exchange for what the =
company claims are deep discounts on product) was a turnoff for many, and a=
 downright headache for others, who criticized the startup=E2=80=99s custom=
er service.=C2=A0

=C2=A0

Anyone who has ever signed up for any sort of product subscription, whether=
 it was the Columbia
Record Club or StyleMint, knows that the proprietors often do everything in=
 their power to make it difficult to unsubscribe. At some point, Savage x F=
enty course-corrected, outlasting many of its competitors in an era when su=
bscriptions were investor catnip. In the end, TechStyle=E2=80=99s early suc=
cess story, the Kate Hudson=E2=80=93fronted Fabletics, eventually scaled by=
 opening physical stores, and now Savage x Fenty is following suit. Drumrig=
ht, who=E2=80=99s worked at TechStyle for more than a
decade, is helping to manage the brand=E2=80=99s growing fleet of stores, w=
hich doubled in 2024 to 14, and wholesale partnerships, like the one launch=
ed with Nordstrom last year.

I don=E2=80=99t know how much Savage x Fenty was valued at in this new fund=
ing round, but I do know that Savage x Fenty=E2=80=99s $1 billion valuation=
 was connected, in part, to the group=E2=80=99s proprietary tech. These day=
s, consumer investors are, for the most part, not interested in proprietary
tech stacks or SaaS products. (Shopify works just fine.) Also, the category=
 is markedly different than it was in 2018 when Savage x Fenty launched. At=
 Skims, Jens and Emma Grede took a page from Savage x Fenty=E2=80=99s inclu=
sivity book and made it hugely profitable with a higher price point and nea=
r-luxury positioning. Rihanna might beat Kim Kardashian in a popularity con=
test, but many would argue that the Skims product is simply better.

And yet, I=E2=80=99m a firm believer that this is not a winner-take-all mar=
ket. Markets can expand, and TechStyle=E2=80=99s do-over might allow Savage=
 x Fenty to scale up fast and with fewer prying eyes. The bigger problem fo=
r Savage x Fenty, I=E2=80=99d say, is that Super knows all of its secrets, =
and these days, she has far more resources at her disposal at VS. Of course=
, TechStyle does have one inimitable ingredient, and that=E2=80=99s Rihanna=
=2E Let=E2=80=99s see whether they can figure out how to finally
use her effectively.

=C2=A0

This piece on the creative director churn makes a lot of good points, but I=
 don=E2=80=99t agree that companies like Kering and LVMH need to be
more patient with new designers. That may have been true 20 years ago, but =
these are enormous businesses now, with serious investors and endless data,=
 and you can=E2=80=99t just dillydally for three or five years. The compani=
es need to minimize risk by ensuring most of the revenue is coming from eve=
rgreenish products that are not designed by the current creative director. =
(See: Louis Vuitton, Herm=C3=A8s, and Chanel as examples.)
[WSJ ( https://www.wsj.com/style/fashion/the-toughest-job-in-fashion-is-top=
-designer-for-a-luxury-brand-7adaaf6b?utm_campaign=3DLine+Sheet+-+SUBSCRIBE=

42abd12a )]

=E2=80=8A

And finally=E2=80=A6 On a recent plane trip, I watched Goodrich, the 2024 f=
ilm
by Hallie Meyers-Shyer, daughter of directors Nancy Meyers (yes) and the la=
te Charles Shyer (yes), and starring Michael Keaton. The movie is fine (I=
=E2=80=99m not a film critic), but=E2=80=94unsurprisingly, given Meyers-Shy=
er=E2=80=99s lineage=E2=80=94it=E2=80=99s really worth watching because of =
how it captures a very particular slice of American upper-middle-class life=
 through its costume and set design.

Keaton=E2=80=99s character owns a gallery, and even
the art is believable, which almost never happens in movies. Anyway, if you=
 want to know how to dress=E2=80=94and also how to dress your kids (neatly,=
 with lots of primary colors, and only one pattern: check)=E2=80=94Goodrich=
 is instructive in the way Nora Ephron movies once were. Every character=E2=
=80=99s wardrobe is incredibly specific and communicates a lot. Also, Mila =
Kunis plays Keaton=E2=80=99s daughter and her hair looks incredible. Anyway=
, start screenshotting!

to me=E2=80=94at Murakami=E2=80=99s current Gagosian show, in London, panel=
s have sold for about $3.5 million apiece. (A rep for Gagosian did not comm=
ent.) Artists in these situations are not typically paid for collaborating,=
 <em>per se</em>, but rather compensated for the artwork
itself. Also, this is technically a =E2=80=9Creedition=E2=80=9D of his prev=
ious designs. I=E2=80=99m told it=E2=80=99s unlikely he=E2=80=99s earning r=
oyalties, but Murakami uses the exposure to sell his own, cheaper, limited-=
edition merch, including pillows, key chains, and other tchotchkes.=C2=A0</=
li><li style=3D"Margin:0 0 24px 0;"><em>Burberry and other London things</e=
m>: London remains the place to scout for young design talent, even if Brex=
it and tightening immigration laws suggest that may not be the case for lon=
g. Chatting with
friends between shows and at the British Fashion Council=E2=80=99s cocktail=
 party on Sunday night, there is concern over the depleted schedule and the=
 lack of good reasons for London Fashion Week=E2=80=99s very existence. (Si=
milar concerns exist in New York, too.) One fellow journalist suggested tha=
t <strong>Phoebe Philo</strong> should show here. That would certainly drum=
 up international interest, but how much?</li></ul></div>

0;">Perhaps it=E2=80=99s okay that the New York and London shows are far mo=
re provincial these days=E2=80=94that=E2=80=99s the nature of the business.=
 More people than ever see the contents of the runway shows on the internet=
, and the remaining store buyers don=E2=80=99t purchase off the runway anym=
ore either. The shows are for catching up, and, for those privileged enough=
 to be able to attend, a good baseline education. It was instructive for me=
 to see both <strong>Emilia Wickstead</strong> and <strong>Simone Rocha</st=
rong>=E2=80=99s
runway propositions in person: Unlike in America, where collections often f=
eel like a mashup of products (driven by the merchant, not the designer), t=
he London shows are realized thematically, with varying degrees of success.=
<br/><br/>Wickstead, known for a certain primness, decided to focus on the =
women behind <strong>Hitchcock</strong>=E2=80=99s <em>The Birds</em>: <stro=
ng>Tippi Hedren</strong>, costume designer <strong>Edith Head</strong>, and=
 <strong>Daphne du Maurier</strong>, who wrote the short
story the movie was based on. Rocha, who said she was feeling =E2=80=9Cemo=
=E2=80=9D while designing this season, manages to make utterly commercial c=
lothes that also feel substantive. The celebrity stunt casting (<strong>Fio=
na Shaw</strong> mostly, but also <strong>Alexa Chung</strong>, <strong>And=
rea Riseborough</strong>, and <strong>Bel Powley</strong>) will get the cli=
cks, but it=E2=80=99s the teenage dream of motorcycle jackets and pink ribb=
ons that will stick. Also, I didn=E2=80=99t make it to <strong>Richard
Quinn</strong>, but I liked what I saw of his bizarro prom dresses online.<=
br/><br/>In London, you=E2=80=99re dealing with <em>real</em> designers=E2=
=80=94some with impressive businesses, others struggling to keep the lights=
 on, but nearly all with genuine ideas that go beyond merchandising tricks.=
 At the Burberry show in a drapery-covered hall at the Tate Britain, the we=
ek=E2=80=99s tentpole, there was very effective, very British stunt casting=
, too: <strong>Richard E. Grant</strong>, <strong>Lesley Manville</strong>,
and a dangerous-looking <strong>Elizabeth McGovern</strong>. (It feels like=
 they=E2=80=99ve all been influenced by last year=E2=80=99s Miu Miu show st=
arring Chung and <strong>Kristin Scott Thomas</strong>.)<br/><br/>Of course=
, all the chatter at the Burberry show was about whether designer <strong>D=
aniel Lee</strong> would stay or go. I=E2=80=99d heard a rumor (I honestly =
can=E2=80=99t remember where) that the collection was going to be all black=
, <em>=C3=A0 la </em><strong>Pierpaolo Piccioli</strong>=E2=80=99s swan son=
g at Valentino.
Instead, we got a near-idyllic Englishwoman=E2=80=99s wardrobe=E2=80=94beau=
tiful fabrics, lots of great outerwear (cropped motos especially), and fabu=
lous over-the-knee boots. At this point, I=E2=80=99m not sure Lee=E2=80=99s=
 fate matters. Burberry went from being an absolute disaster to something r=
espectable, inching toward what it=E2=80=99s <em>supposed</em> to be: the f=
irst word in rain gear, and then some. Whatever happens, it=E2=80=99s clear=
ly turned a corner. =C2=A0</li></ul></div>

ilable in a much broader range of sizes than competitors. Almost immediatel=

ign:left;mso-line-height-alt:25.5px;"><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0p=
x;">Unlike Fenty Beauty, Savage x Fenty=E2=80=99s first-mover status into l=
arger-size intimate
apparel did <em>not</em> make it an instant success. The venture-backed Tec=
hStyle=E2=80=99s membership model (users pay a monthly fee in exchange for =
what the company claims are deep discounts on product) was a turnoff for ma=
ny, and a downright headache for others, who criticized the startup=E2=80=
=99s customer service.=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=
=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">Anyone who has ever sign=
ed up for any sort of product subscription, whether it was the Columbia
Record Club or StyleMint, knows that the proprietors often do everything in=
 their power to make it difficult to unsubscribe. At some point, Savage x F=
enty course-corrected, outlasting many of its competitors in an era when su=
bscriptions were investor catnip. In the end, TechStyle=E2=80=99s early suc=
cess story, the <strong>Kate Hudson</strong>=E2=80=93fronted Fabletics, eve=
ntually scaled by opening physical stores, and now Savage x Fenty is follow=
ing suit. Drumright, who=E2=80=99s worked at TechStyle for more than a
decade, is helping to manage the brand=E2=80=99s growing fleet of stores, w=
hich doubled in 2024 to 14, and wholesale partnerships, like the one launch=
ed with Nordstrom last year.<br/><br/></p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-botto=
m:0px;">I don=E2=80=99t know how much Savage x Fenty was valued at in this =
new funding round, but I do know that Savage x Fenty=E2=80=99s $1 billion v=
aluation was connected, in part, to the group=E2=80=99s proprietary tech. T=
hese days, consumer investors are, for the most part, not interested in pro=
prietary
tech stacks or SaaS products. (Shopify works just fine.) Also, the category=
 is markedly different than it was in 2018 when Savage x Fenty launched. At=
 Skims, <strong>Jens</strong> and <strong>Emma Grede</strong> took a page f=
rom Savage x Fenty=E2=80=99s inclusivity book and made it hugely profitable=
 with a higher price point and near-luxury positioning. Rihanna might beat =
<strong>Kim Kardashian</strong> in a popularity contest, but many would arg=
ue that the Skims product is simply better. <br/><br/></p><p style=3D"margi=
n:0;">And yet, I=E2=80=99m a firm believer that this is <em>not </em>a winn=
er-take-all market. Markets can expand, and TechStyle=E2=80=99s do-over mig=
ht allow Savage x Fenty to scale up fast and with fewer prying eyes. The bi=
gger problem for Savage x Fenty, I=E2=80=99d say, is that Super knows all o=
f its secrets, and these days, she has far more resources at her disposal a=
t VS. Of course, TechStyle does have one inimitable ingredient, and that=E2=
=80=99s Rihanna. Let=E2=80=99s see whether they can figure out how to final=
ly
use her effectively.</p></div>
